one_line_takeaway: SeqMark fixes 'region collapse' in sequence-level LM watermarking by mean-centering high-probability output embeddings before LSH partitioning, substantially improving watermark detection F1 (e.g., XSum F1(h) 89.7 vs SemStamp's 60.4) on low-entropy constrained-generation tasks without sacrificing output quality.
key_finding: On WMT19 sentence translation, SeqMark achieves F1(h)=77.1 and F1(nw)=79.0 versus SemStamp's 65.9/65.5, k-SemStamp's 43.4/43.1, KGW's 45.7/47.6, and SWEET's 39.5/39.6, while COMET quality is comparable across methods (SeqMark 87.1 vs No-Watermark 87.4). On XSum summarization, SeqMark reaches F1(h)=89.7 and F1(nw)=85.3 (best among all baselines, whose best is SemStamp at 60.4/63.1), with ROUGE-L 21.6 (slightly above the 20.5 no-watermark baseline). On WMT23 paragraph translation, SeqMark attains AUROC/TP@1/TP@5=100/100/100 for human-negatives detection versus KGW's 72.1/11.0/25.0 and SemStamp's 84.0/30.0/40.0, with COMET 87.7 matching SemStamp. On MBPP code generation, SeqMark gets F1(h)=80.7 (vs SWEET's 70.2, SemStamp's 69.2, KGW's 45.7) while maintaining pass@1=33.6, close to the No-Watermark 33.8 (KGW drops pass@1 to 15.0). The Table 1 region-entropy/cosine-similarity diagnostic shows SeqMark achieves 0.81-0.96 region entropy with 0.01-0.03 cosine similarity across translation, summary, and open-ended tasks, versus SemStamp's 0.19-0.71 entropy / 0.31-0.97 similarity and k-SemStamp's 0.11-0.87 entropy / 0.31-0.97 similarity, empirically confirming region collapse in the baselines and its mitigation in SeqMark. The abstract's headline claim of 'up to 28% increase in F1' is consistent with the summarization F1(h) gain (89.7 vs SemStamp's 60.4, a roughly 29-point absolute gain). Ablation (Table 5) shows mean-centering and closest-to-mean transformations perform best (COMET 85.0/85.4, F1(h) 80.2/80.6) while subtracting a random embedding performs worst (F1(h) 27.7), confirming the transformation choice matters. Fast-SeqMark (Table 6) trades some detection performance (F1(h) 71.4 vs SeqMark's 80.2) for not requiring LM access at detection time, but still outperforms KGW and SemStamp.
core_contribution: The paper (Le, Ritter, Goyal; Georgia Tech) shows that existing LM watermarking schemes fail on low-entropy, constrained-generation tasks (machine translation, summarization, code generation) and introduces SeqMark, a sequence-level watermarking algorithm with 'semantic differentiation.' SeqMark first isolates the manifold of high-probability admissible outputs for a prompt by low-temperature sampling n embeddings, then applies a mean-centering transformation f(c_i;C) = c_i - mean(C) before locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) partitions the transformed space into red/green regions. This spreads semantically similar high-quality outputs across partitions instead of collapsing them into one region, which is the paper's diagnosed failure mode in prior sequence-level watermarks. A companion variant, Fast-SeqMark, trains a neural network (a finetuned LaBSE encoder) to predict the mean embedding directly from the prompt, avoiding the need for LM access at detection time.
threat_model: Provenance/attribution setting: a language-model provider wants to embed an imperceptible, statistically detectable marker into generated text so it can later be distinguished from human-written text or from non-watermarked/other-LM text, even when the task's output space is highly constrained (translation, summarization, code). The paper explicitly names downstream misuse risks in its Limitations: (1) spoofing attacks, where an adversary embeds a stolen or guessed watermark signature into unethical/harmful text to falsely attribute it to a specific model provider, and (2) the practical need to keep the LSH hash function key secret to prevent forgery. It does not model adversarial removal beyond a limited paraphrasing-attack probe (Appendix A.5).
limitations: As stated by the authors: (1) SeqMark requires access to the underlying LM at detection time to regenerate samples for computing the mean embedding, a nontrivial deployment cost partially mitigated by Fast-SeqMark at a detection-accuracy cost; (2) unlike SemStamp/k-SemStamp, SeqMark may be more susceptible to paraphrasing attacks because it deliberately spreads similar generations across regions, though a preliminary paraphrase-attack experiment (Appendix A.5) found current paraphrasers degrade text quality too much on low-entropy tasks like translation to be a practical evasion; (3) the method assumes highly probable LM generations are high-quality, which the authors acknowledge 'might not always be the case'; (4) the dual-use risk of spoofing/misattribution is explicitly flagged, with the mitigation being to keep the LSH hash key secret.

quantitative_results: WMT19 translation: SeqMark F1(h)=77.1, F1(nw)=79.0, COMET=87.1 vs No-Watermark COMET=87.4
XSum summarization: SeqMark F1(h)=89.7, F1(nw)=85.3, ROUGE-L=21.6, COMET=68.5
WMT23 paragraph translation: SeqMark AUROC/TP@1/TP@5(h)=100/100/100, BLEU/COMET=39.8/87.7
MBPP code generation: SeqMark F1(h)=80.7, pass@1=33.6 vs No-Watermark pass@1=33.8

Region entropy / cosine similarity (Table 1): SeqMark 0.81/0.01 (translation), 0.92/0.03 (summary), 0.96/0.01 (open-ended) vs SemStamp 0.19/0.97, 0.39/0.92, 0.71/0.59 and k-SemStamp 0.25/0.97, 0.11/0.94, 0.87/0.31
Abstract-claimed headline: up to 28% increase in F1 for watermark detection
Fast-SeqMark on WMT19: F1(h)=71.4, COMET=84.4 (vs SeqMark F1(h)=80.2, COMET=85.0; both still beat KGW's 47.8 and SemStamp's 65.5)
